text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic myomectomy on a ten-centimeter uterine myoma, using uterine cannulation for exposure. A vertical hystereotomy is made near the midline with a monopolar needle or harmonic scalpel, avoiding vascular areas and protecting the fallopian tube.

The surgeon performs intracapsular enucleation of the uterine myoma, carefully dissecting the plane between the myoma and the myometrium using scissors, a monopolar needle, or a harmonic scalpel. Minimal coagulation is applied to prevent necrosis at the incision edges.

The surgeon inspects the surgical site to ensure the endometrial cavity remains closed after enucleation. If opened, the cavity is closed with a two-layer suture using intracorporeal knots with absorbable material to prevent hemorrhagic complications and reduce intrauterine adhesions. The edges are carefully re-approximated, and figurinate sutures are applied if hemorrhagic conditions are present.
The surgeon continues to perform cross stitches using absorbable suture on the uterine wall, demonstrating the technique with options for running sutures using Veloc or StrataFix.

The surgeon uses a contained bag morcellation technique to extract the myoma, inserting the bag within approximately nine minutes. An anti-adhesion agent is applied to the surgical site to minimize adhesion formation post-operation.
